The picture is of Walter Gibson (1897-1985), one of the great authors of the pulp magazine era, creator of "the Shadow," who confounds one of the common assumptions  of the contemporary faux-skeptic movement by having been both a first-rate stage magician and a practicing occultist. If you can find his book The Complete Illustrated Book of the Psychic Sciences -- written with his wife Litzka -- you'll find a good solid overview of popular occultism from just before the late 20th century occult revival.

And if you're wondering who knows what evil lurks in the hearts of men...the Shadow knows!

comments.title: Cue eerie whistling
comments.content:
You've mentioned before that one should never do energy work such as circulating the light except in a space that one has protected and (I suppose) consecrated with the LBRP or some similar ritual. You've also said that in public situations such as in the doctor's waiting room or at the bus stop is acceptable to meditate.

In his book "Elementary Psionics" Charles Cosimano advises the reader to visualize the aura while meditating; he also suggests visualizing a column of light descending from above into the aura and charging it up, so to speak, while doing so. He presents this as a preparation for doing psionic workings. In your opinion, is this too much in the nature of energy work to be done safely outside of one's private and ritually protected space? Is there some clearly defined point where we can draw the line between meditation and energy work as described above, and if so where is it?

You also mentioned recently that one can meditate in any physical position. This seems like good news to me. I fairly often encounter the situation in which I would like to meditate, am not yet ready for sleep, and yet am so physically exhausted that I yearn to lie down. Are there any protocols one needs to know about for doing this properly? Is orientation to the cardinal points critical, or can one face in any direction?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Cue eerie whistling
replies.content:
1) No, there's no hard and fast line. It depends partly on the condition of your own aura, partly on other personal characteristics including whether or not you have a relationship with a deity or other protective beings such as helpful ancestors, and partly on the astral and etheric condition of the spaces where you're doing such work. Forming what some sources call an "astral egg" is a fairly common bit of beginner's magic, and I'm not at all surprised to see Uncle Chuckie including it in his book. (I disagree with some of his views but the guy's technically very competent.)

2) Most people find that meditating in a lying-down position is a fast ticket to sleep. If that's not true for you, then by all means do so. No, there are no particular rules as far as I know.

replies.title: circulating the light
replies.content:
I was taught a method of circulating the light called "running energy". My teacher had been trained by the Berkeley Psychic Institute, which was and probably still is operating under the umbrella of The Church of Divine Man.

The method I was taught has similarities (as well as differences) with the Sphere of Protection operation as described on this blog, the preparations for discursive meditation ditto, and what Anonymous describes as Charles Cosimano's directions.

I have been doing this method (as best I remember it; I wasn't an apt student) at irregular intervals for decades. Summarizing it, one sits in a chair upright with hands on legs, keeps one's base of consciousness between the ears (the direction is "Stay in the center of your head"), visualizes sending a grounding cord from one of the lower two chakras (can't remember which) to the center of the earth, draws "earth energy" up the cord, circulates the energy up the central axis of the body, throughout the body, and out the palms, soles of feet and the top of the head. The energy is described IIRC as reddish brown and "85 percent". One then draws in "15 percent gold cosmic energy" through the crown chakra, down the central axis of the body (I think the original directions specify front or back of spine in the manner of kundalini yoga, but that was too complex for me so I didn't pay attention) and circulating the energy throughout the body and out in the same manner. After that, one does some maintenance work on the aura.

I don't remember any particular instructions about when and where to run energy. There might have been some, but all I recall is a subsequent warning from my teacher not to do it while driving a car. I believe this was a warning about distraction.

It seems as if there are a lot of variant versions of circulating the light.

replies.title: Re: circulating the light
replies.content: Good heavens, now there's a blast from the past. I knew a bunch of people from the CDM in Seattle when I was in my teens; if I'd had the money I would have taken their classes.

comments.title: The Sacred
comments.content:
Hello!

Dear JMG

What would be your occult / magical definition or understanding of what sacredness is?

Many thanks

Regards

replies.title: Re: The Sacred
replies.content:
I'm always baffled about what to say in response to questions like this. "Sacred" is a word -- that is, it's a verbal noise made by a bunch of primates, to which they each associate various experiences they've had or heard about from others. The definition of a word is a collection of other verbal noises made when the primates in question get confused, because they figure out they're using the word as a label for different experiences, and try to find some way of talking about the word that they can agree on.

"Sacred" isn't a verbal noise I use much, precisely because no two of us primates seem to mean the same thing by it. Sitting around grunting and hooting with my fellow primates, as we try to figure out a way of talking about a verbal noise that we can all agree on, can be an interesting experience sometimes, but especially in cases like this -- where there's nothing we can all point to and say "Uhhn! Uhhn!" so we all know we're grunting about the same thing -- I tend to give it a pass.

replies.content: A grunt expressing that my mind found out this extremely funny had to come out of me. My fellow primate colleagues got startled. I wonder if scenes like that are what omnipresent beings consider Animal Planet.

comments.title: solar paths, telluric paths, and lunar paths,
comments.content:
Hi!
Can you elaborate a bit more on what is meant by these different paths( solar paths, telluric paths, and lunar paths,)? Are they mutually exclusive for instance?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: solar paths, telluric paths, and lunar paths,
replies.content:
In certain traditions of Druid magic, there are said to be three basic currents of subtle energy that mages and mystics can use in their work; the names used for them in these traditions are the solar current, the telluric current, and the lunar current. The solar current includes all astrological influences (in these traditions, all such influences are held to be modifications of the influence of the sun). The telluric current includes all influences that rise from within the earth, including those that make some places holy and others malign. The solar current waxes, wanes, and changes in quality according to time, the telluric current waxes, wanes, and changes in quality according to place.

The lunar current is different. While the solar and telluric currents are always present, the lunar current has to be made out of the fusion of solar and telluric forces. Certain natural objects and phenomena create the lunar current now and then, but if you want to work with it reliably you need to learn ways to generate it. Some ancient structures, such as standing stones, are part of archaic systems meant to generate it; there are also ritual and meditative means of doing so.

All that's necessary to understand the distinction between the three paths. Some systems of magic work primarily with the descending energies of the solar current -- the Golden Dawn system is a good example. Some systems of magic work primarily with the rising energies of the telluric current -- the magical system taught by RJ Stewart in various formats is a good example. Some systems work primarily with the lunar current, and focus on practices that generate it -- the system taught in my book The Druid Magic Handbook is a good example. None of these is better than the others -- it's purely a matter of what energy you want to work with, and how.

replies.title: (no subject)

2) She's messing with you -- something most trad occult writers do to keep students from taking the symbolism too rigidly. If you use that as a theme for meditation, you'll grasp some aspects of the planes you might otherwise miss -- and you'll also have a keener sense of why most of the time, a different correspondence fits better.

comments.title: Immunisations
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Hot topic I know, but what are the magical impacts of immunisations on people/children (aside from immunity!)? (Would you get your kids immunised?) And if so, what else might you do? Or if not, what other steps would you take?

Our 10 week old son is facing a about 10 different immunisations next week. We’re going to get most of them, but wanted your thoughts from a magical perspective.

Many thanks
Edward

replies.title: Re: Immunisations
replies.content:
That's not something I've looked into, but I don't think it's likely to have any significant magical dimension. Human beings, like other living things, are constantly absorbing genetic material from outside ourselves -- about ten per cent of your body weight consists of other living things inside you, for example. So immunization is simply another mode of that natural process.

If I'd had living children, they would have gotten immunizations for a few really serious conditions such as diphtheria and tetanus, but the rest, not a chance -- it's not exactly comforting to me that vaccine manufacturers in the US are legally shielded from lawsuits for harm caused by their products. I'm unconvinced by the claims that vaccines cause autism, but I've encountered enough cases of definite harm caused by vaccines that I wouldn't take the risk -- and the attitude of those who insist that you must never question the pharmaceutical industry or its pimps in the medical profession strikes me as equal parts stupidity and rank corruption.

comments.title: Overbanishing
comments.content:
The Golden Dawn and related groups seem to have this habit of banishing everything they've meticulously invoked at the end of a ceremony (e.g., the reverse circumambulation and banishing rituals at the close of the watchrower ceremony). What are your thoughts on this? Does this not simply undo the work you've done? Compared to a general license to depart one finds in the computer it's always seemed a little excessive to me. What would be the potential consequences of replacing all of these banishing ceremonies at the end of a ritual with a general license to depart?

Thanks for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Overbanishing
replies.content: If you're baking a cake, let's say, you get out various ingredients and tools, measure and mix and pop the cake in the oven, and then you put the flour and the sugar and the butter back where they belong, and put the bowl and the other tools you used in the sink to be washed; if you don't do that, your kitchen quickly gets too cluttered to use, the butter may go rancid, and mold can get going in the mixing bowl. Banishing after a Golden Dawn ritual serves exactly the same function. You've invoked a range of specialized energies at high intensity, using potent ritual formulae, and a general license to depart won't send them back where they belong; you need to banish what you've invoked, so you and the space you're in both return to a balanced condition, and you can call on other forces the next time you do magic. Of course you don't put the cake back into the cupboard -- you serve it and eat it! -- but there are specific ways in the GD system to make sure that the energies you've invoked go to the purpose you have in mind, and aren't disspelled by the banishings you use to close.

comments.title: Illness and Magic
comments.content:
My mother, who has been working on LRM for a few years now has had a rather sudden bout of health problems and is currently in the hospital for a few days. At one point there was a brief heart scare, and she was in tears over the possibility of having to stop the LBRP since it makes her feel safe and protected and I think she was more afraid of the idea of living without it than she was of whatever diagnosis came her way. (Fortunately it looks like nothing that’s going on is likely to be serious or permanent, it’s a bacterial infection from a medicine side effect and should be quickly treated and didn’t involve her heart, so giving up magic isn’t an issue at this point, but it did get us talking). On her behalf a few questions:

1: One of the more common warnings you see in books on ritual Magic is that it shouldn’t be practiced by individuals with certain health conditions. One of the big ones is heart conditions. Considering that some form of heart condition is among the most common health problems that manifest late in life, what sorts of adjustments to daily practice would you recommend / would you as a life-long Magician yourself make if one of those health conditions arose? Does that warning primarily just refer to more intense magical workings while still allowing for the core life-long daily practices like banishing, meditation, and divination? Is this a situation where something like the sphere of protection might be gentler and safer than a form of the LBRP? Or do you really have to completely quit a lifetime of practice with the onset of certain common old age illnesses at a time when such practices might be more crucial than ever?

2: How do you recommend handling daily banishing rituals when stuck in a hospital bed for a day or two? Can it be done as an inner plane working if you can’t physically get up and move around?

replies.title: Re: Illness and Magic
replies.content:
1) This is something that occult schools have been having to deal with for a long time. One of the reasons I recommend the SoP is precisely that it's easier on the subtle and physical bodies than the LBRP, and so can be done regularly by people who probably shouldn't risk the more strenuous forms of ritual magic. Most people can keep up a daily banishing -- either the SoP, or the LBRP plus Middle Pillar -- into advanced old age despite minor heart trouble or the like -- it's the more advanced magical practices that can be problematic in that case. As for meditation and divination, those are perfectly safe to continue; if anything, meditation should be practiced more intensively as you get older, because it's good for the heart and the physical body generally.

You can also choose to keep on doing magical practices knowing that it may shorten your life a bit. You're going to die anyway, after all, and it really does come down to deciding how you want to spend the finite number of hours you have left.

2) Visualize it. Imagine yourself getting up out of the hospital bed, standing in the middle of the room, and doing the banishing, just as though you were doing it in your physical body. This is excellent training anyway, as it builds the capacity to visualize and concentrate, but it's especially useful when you're bedridden -- and with practice, the effect can be as powerful as doing it in your physical body.

replies.title: Re: Illness and Magic
replies.content: In what ways is the SOP easier on the subtle and physical bodies? I wonder about switching to it, as I've been doing the LBRP/Central Ray for a while now, and having trouble keeping it up.

replies.title: Re: Illness and Magic
replies.content: I'm not sure I can explain it -- it's simply been my experience, and that of other practitioners, that the SoP is gentler in its effects.

comments.title: Update on last week's 'enemy' question

A minor incident sent me to the Gypsy Witch cards determined to pin things down, once and for all. The first thing I asked them was "Has my behavior offended anybody?" I got a definite No. The next two involved the minor incident, easily corrected. Again, No. The next 3 or 4 referred to specific people or groups of people. Again, No. Finally I asked "It is a clash of values?" and got a most decided Yes.

Then, being at that moment, more perceptive than usual, I asked "Are my values misguided?" And got an answer, less from the cards than from the figures on my altar, "you do have a tendency to blow things out of proportion." So!

P.S. The incident involved one of those interminable surveys I had filled out and not sent in yet. I merely called the person issuing them to say "I filled out the survey when I was not feeling well and it came out very badly. [Full of errors, handwriting totally illegible, I wasn't about to say, covered with snarky comments.] Could I please have a blank copy? Done - filled out honestly, sensibly, and legibly. So problem. Just FYI.

So.... pinpointing the problem with a series of small and very specific questions did the job.

Pat in Gainesville.

A quarter-century ago, when I was working up my old course on the history of women-led magical religions in the USA, something of that sort emerged from the raw data: there were periods of about 35-40 years when alternative religions emerged and flourished, alternating with periods of much the same length when these new religious movements were challenged by the authorities. A cultural "pendulum," as it were, appeared to swing between permissive and repressive extremes--if you will, between Chesed and Gevurah. Eventually I looked in to Strauss and Howe's theory of generations to see whether there was anything of the same sort there, biut I found it to be far too eleborate and complex to either convince me or line up all that neatly with the pendulum-swing I had observed. In cycles related to economics there is the Kondratieff Wave which oscillates, guess what, at every 40 to 60 years.
There is also the work of Martin Armstrong.
It makes me wonder about connections between astrological conditions and these observed cycles.

replies.title: Re: Mercury retrograde shadow
replies.content: Mercury retrograde is one of the big bad fooftawoos of contemporary astrology, and in my opinion it's been massively overblown. Retrogradation is a debility, but it's less of one than (say) detriment, fall, or a square or opposition with a malefic. If you slow down with them, it all works out fine; if you try to rush things at such times, yeah, you're going to have some problems. My guess is that that's what's behind it.

The reason I ask is that for the past year or so, I’ve been practicing the Polytheist LBRP with Irish deities, with decent results. I also have a committed devotional relationship with a number of the Tuatha Dé. However, since about March, Jesus has started to be present in my life in a way that hasn’t been the case in almost fifteen years, and since June I’ve become very involved in a local Episcopal parish. As a Christian Druid, I’m looking for ways to incorporate both my worship of the Holy Trinity and my reverence for the panoply of gods into my daily practice. Both personal experience and extensive divination has shown that They work in much greater harmony than, ahem, the propaganda would seem to suggest, and particularly the gods of Ireland seem especially conducive to operating in that liminal context between the ancient pagan world and the Christian culture that developed and thrived on that soil.

My thought was to invoke the Trinity for the Opening and Irish gods for the seven Elements. I thought though it would be best to ask for advice before going about experimenting recklessly!

In any case, I want to thank you and everyone here for these weekly conversations. It was in very large part the failure of the Church to teach competently and to refrain from making unrealistic promises about a young Christian’s spiritual power and the tangible presence of God that led me to shuffle reluctantly away from the Church out of a sense of abandonment and despair, when more than a decade ago the shale collided with the cooling device and I started experiencing some truly horrifying and undeniably real supernatural encounters, which left wounds (spiritual and physical) that still have not fully healed. (The recent discussions of trends towards demonism and diabolic witchcraft have hit very close to home; I did not seek those experiences out, and I cannot but stare in horrified astonishment at anyone who would.) Likewise, it was in large part the community here that showed the path to reconcile my Druidry with an irrepressibly reviving Christianity, and for that I am enormously grateful.

replies.title: Re: Finishing spells & such
replies.content:
1) Once you have the job and have been going to work for a week or more, thank the energies you've invoked and bury the materials in your back yard, so the effects will stay with you. (You bury something in your front yard to make it go away.)

2) Okay, you're mixing up several different things here. Planetary charity consists of making donations to appropriate charities on the day and hour of the planet -- for example, donating to a wolf sanctuary on the day and hour of Mars. That's what it is, and it won't mess up -- I've never heard of anyone having a negative reaction here.

What you're doing with the candles is not planetary charity, it's worship. It's a very good thing to do, and it doesn't surprise me that the goddess Venus is responding -- by all accounts she's one of the most approachable of the planetary deities, and every deity likes to be thanked for the blessings they bestow. Just keep on doing what you're doing -- lighting a candle and thanking the deity -- and her blessings will keep on coming your way; that's the classic way to invoke a deity, and it works.

comments.title: Ceres
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I started studying The Cosmic Doctrine about a month ago following your posts on Ecosophia and it has both been going well and feeling right. In the past week, I have added daily practice of the Sphere of Protection using your posts here on Dreamwidth (I am currently still just opening and closing) and meditation, also following your posts here on Dreamwidth (I am doing the sitting and just recently started the second step of relaxation).

Obviously, this is still pretty new, though I’ve dabbled a bit in the past with discursive meditation as outlined in The Druidry Handbook and have been reading portions of your occult writings for years.

I had an experience on Saturday and am trying to decide how best to react and move forward with it. I performed the opening and closing of the Sphere of Protection. The ritual felt more powerful on Saturday than it had in previous ones, though I have found from my second performance of it that I do feel a strong reaction to the ritual, particularly with the third step of the opening, when I picture the light rising up from the earth, and with the closing (the meeting of the two currents at my solar plexus feels very powerful). In the back of my mind, I was thinking about this as I did the ritual and thinking about how the earth currents seem to impact me more. I could also note that I am heavy on earth signs in my natal chart (and if I’m remembering correctly, very light on air signs) and that in general I have always felt a strong tie to the earth--that that is always the element that has most captured me, so to speak.

I’ll also note that I have been using the more general phrasing of “By the sky above me,” “by the earth beneath me,” “by the spirit within me,” and “may I be blessed and renewed now and always.” I considered using your suggested Hu/Ced/Hesus configuration since I am still interested in the druid path, but I did not feel a strong connection to or desire to invoke those gods.

After I performed the SOP on Saturday, I did my meditation sitting. After a few minutes of sitting, my vision became very unfocused and I started to feel in a trance state. This wasn’t too different than how I have felt with the SOP and at other times in my life, so nothing too strange, but it was fairly intense. After perhaps a minute or less, I heard an “I’ve been waiting for you” within my head, presumably from some other entity (I understood this as a statement about my taking a long part of my life to find and pursue this path). I also immediately questioned if I was just creating this scenario for myself rather than actually touching base with a separate entity. I had the thought that I shouldn’t be full of myself thinking that some entity or deity was “waiting” for me, as I’m not that special.

I immediately felt a slap back from the entity in question that contained maybe not exactly the words but the pretty clear response that I should not engage in false modesty and the implication that I should also not be egotistical, with the somewhat annoyed assurance that this entity was perfectly capable of paying attention to plenty of people at once. I then wondered who this entity was and got the name Ceres.

At the moment, I (incorrectly) thought this was the earth goddess you mentioned in your Dreamwidth post, so I still had a question if I was not just inserting a god’s name and imagining this scenario, something of a wishful thinking. However, I did not explicitly reject the experience based on the previous pushback and a growing sense that there was something real behind this. I remained in the trance-like phase and there was a sort of webbing over my unfocused vision that I started to notice was rhythmically moving back and forth in an approximation of breathing. I tied that experience to Ceres.

At this point, my five minute alarm went off (I need to get a clock set up, but currently am using a timer). For a moment, I pretty clearly was told by Ceres to ignore the alarm and so I did for perhaps a half minute or so, not that long. Then I thought I received instruction to turn it off, but was a little unsure as I did, and apologized if I had done anything wrong. I then sat for another minute or so as the trance wore off and I kind of shook myself back into the present world.

I was still under the impression that Ceres was the god you mentioned in your SOP write up, so I checked afterward and realized it was actually Ced. Intrigued, I did a quick search for Ceres and discovered, as I’m sure you’re well aware, that she was a Roman goddess of agriculture and fertility. I actually laughed when I saw this as it seems a bit too perfect; if I had to pick any one subject and practice that most interests and captivates me, it is probably growing food and eating it. I have worked on small farms, get renewal from gardening, and my professional life is related to the local food movement.

All that said (apologies for the length) I have a couple quick questions if you’re willing to weigh in:

(1) Does this sound in line with a TSW moment for me? Part of me thinks it happened awfully fast, but ultimately it seems legit to me. I don’t think I was just imagining it, but would be curious if the experience sounds in line with something you would expect.

(2) If Ceres really has taken an interest in me and I wanted to somehow integrate that into my practices going forward, do you have recommendations for how best to do that, or suggested reading? Ways to honor her? Pitfalls to avoid? For the moment, I plan to continue to practice the SOP and meditate, continuing to work my way up to full practices over the coming weeks/months following your guides here on Dreamwidth. I don’t want to rush anything and be deliberate in building this. What is the best way for me to integrate Ceres into this, or should I not worry about that for now? (I already had a powerful SOP practice on Sunday and a secondary experience during meditation that involved an unplanned kind of color breathing [green] that also felt tied to Ceres.)

(3) When one is contacted by a deity, is there some kind of study around that you recommend? Should I try to find some good reading sources about Ceres to learn more?

Thanks for any help or guidance you can provide. I didn’t see anything specific in the Magic Monday FAQ about this, so hopefully I’m not asking too repetitive a question. And feel free to point me to one of your books (or someone else’s) if there’s one or a section that would be particularly useful for me to read!

replies.content:

2) Don't worry about that for now. You might pray to her and ask for her guidance in your practices, then follow whatever guidance comes your way.

If someone works around and handles dead bodies or parts of dead bodies (such as a tissue bank), are there any additional spiritual precautions they should take?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You don't specify which tradition you practice, but Judaism takes coming into contact with a dead body very seriously. The dead body itself is never left alone, but is always with two or more people. Since burial is rarely the same day, pairs of people sit with the body in shifts, reciting hymns and prayers. (The folks who do this are called the Chevra Kadisha, the holy society, and it's usually a volunteer position that is considered very difficult but honorable.)

After contact with the dead body, the living are supposed to bathe, then immerse in the mikveh (a ritual bath that must be connected to the ground and fed at least in part by rainwater). They usually attend some kind of community prayer session soon afterwards with a minyan (10 adult Jews) in which the Amidah and the Mourners Kaddish are recited (prayers that function kind of like banishing rituals, although contemporary Judaism doesn't use that term.)

I defer to our host's expertise, but personally if I was working with/near the recently dead on a regular basis, I would attend a daily minyan or perform a daily banishing, and immerse in the mikveh or take a hoodoo bath regularly.

-Ms. Krieger

comments.title: geomancy and inner/outer elements

replies.title: Re: Astrology (II)
replies.content:
You can work yourself into a very fine swivet trying to get the details of an astrological chart down to extreme exactitude. Since most hospital staff rarely get a birth time written down accurately -- they've got other things to do just then, such as dealing with a brand new baby and the mom! -- it's usually a waste of time. If you're lucky, your birth time will be accurate to within maybe ten minutes, and the various algorithms for working out sidereal time are better than that but not perfect; if your rising sign is accurate to within a degree or so of the actual, you're in luck. There are various ways to check, which you'll find covered in the chapters on rectifying a chart, but the basic rule is don't worry about it.

Seriously, don't worry about it. Work out your natal chart as best you can, get the house cusps and planetary placements to within a degree, and leave it at that. Astrology was created at a time when there were no timepieces at all, and the best you had to go by was looking up the altitude of the sun, moon, or an important star in a table keyed for the latitude and season. It still worked very well, and it still works very well now.

replies.title: diagonals on flags
replies.content:
I had not come across that before.

The largest design features of the flag of the United States are the horizontal stripes and the rectangular field of the stars.

From 1789 until some time before the Civil War, the arrangement of the stars on the field was not standardized, partly because states were rapidly admitted and perhaps because flags were hand sewn. Maybe people didn't think having uniformity in the arrangement of the stars was very important.

In early 1912, New Mexico and Arizona were admitted to the Union in rapid succession. By that time we in the era of uniform machine sewn flags. For the next 46 years, the American flag had 48 stars arranged in six horizontal rows of eight stars. All the design elements other than the stars themselves were vertical or horizontal. I grew up with this flag and it was easy to remember exactly how it looked.

On 1/3/1959, Alaska became a state. The 49 star flag was easy to arrange 7 x 7, but we didn't have it long, because on 8/21/1959, Hawaii became a state. This presented a problem for star arrangement. 50 is not a prime number, but its divisors do not work out well as lines in a nearly square rectangle. The flag designers were forced to stagger lines of different numbers of stars. This produces a field of stars that have horizontal lines and diagonal lines, but no verticals.

We have been a 50 state country now for 60 years. We will have to lose some states or gain some states to get back to neat rows.

On page 279 of _The Shoggoth Concerto_ you write: "Then there was number, which was a complete mystery to Sho. It made no sense to her that two books had some quality in common with two two cans of soup or two eyes, and the more Brecken tried to explain, the more confused Sho got."

To my mind this passage does a good of illustrating the property of cardinal numbers, numbers in the abstract; two as an abstract quality that pervades all things that have one more than one.

There is another type of number, the ordinal, which is number as sequence. Not one, two, three, but rather first, second, third. "Second" is very different structurally than the abstraction of "two" but they can be understood analogously.

It seems to me, then, that Shoggoths with a musical language would have a very keep understanding of ordinal numbers, although their bodies would not allow for cardinal understanding perhaps. Indeed, all systematic understandings rely on nested sequences; think of a musician practicing piano by the hour. The piano is the ordinal instrument par excellence with the notes sequenced in relation right there on the keyboard, the analog clock the supreme ordinal metric of time, as the relational aspects of time are laid right out on the face of it. The composition being played is the ordinal sequence of notes, ordered then onto the sequence of the keyboard and the sequence of clock time. Planetary hours represent another ordinal sequence, as does astrology in general. Astrology could be said to be the study of the ordinal sequence of planetary motion.

Is it fair to say, then, that ordinal numbers are the stuff of operative magic? The ordinal used as sequences to change the structure of consciousness in accordance to will, and that cardinal numbers are the stuff of mysticism, the art of union with the Real? In this sense then, would it be fair to say that the Tree of Life is mystical when worked with cardinally and magical when worked with ordinally?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Interesting. The classic occult distinction isn't between cardinal and ordinal numbers, but between numbers as quantities and numbers as qualities. To the occult student of number, three isn't just a quantity, it's a condition of "threeness" with its own intellectual and emotional "flavor," which is expressed best in symbolism -- for example, the symbols assigned to Binah on the Tree of Life. What makes a planetary hour magical isn't so much its place in a sequence but its flavor, as distinctive and indescribable as the flavor of the petit madeleine cookie that plays so famous a role in the opening chapter of Proust's Remembrance of Things Past.

Shoggoths (as I imagine them) may have an instinctive sense of ordinality, but they can't count time, or anything else. Their language of whistled notes is inborn -- the Elder Things programmed it into their genetic structure -- and so they have no more conscious awareness of its mathematical structure than you have of the chemicals your stomach so assiduously synthesizes to digest your breakfast every single morning. ;-)

replies.title: assignment of correspondences to plants
replies.content:
The ideas I'm suggesting here are speculative. They might be things you have already taken into consideration.

I took an introductory class in Western Herbalism, the sort of herbalism that John Lust's and Matthew Wood's books are based on. The class I took included both practice and theory. As I'm sure you know, Western herbalism categorizes plants by binary qualities like hot/cold, wet/dry and about a dozen other pairs of qualities. My impression is that herbalists arrived at these classifications at least partly by sensory observation and working with them. For example, our teacher passed around tinctures of single plants, we tasted a drop of one tincture at a time and reported our impressions. For some tinctures, I only noticed the taste. A short period of time after ingesting a few of these tinctures, I felt as if I was having a hot flash. These were tinctured from plants that the herbals listed as hot.

Since planets, elements and zodiacal signs have some of these qualities associated with them, that might be a source of attribution.

Medical uses would be another. Plants that affect a function, organ or disease associated with a planet would get that planet as a ruler.

In the chapters of The White Goddess in which he discusses the plants that make up his version of the Celtic Tree Calendar, Robert Graves gives a variety of kinds of information to support assigning a plant to a month, including its growing habits, human uses of the plant such as a dye of a particular color, and its mythological associations. The CTC appears to postdate the classical herbals; the methods by which Graves constructed his system of correspondences are older than the calendar itself.

comments.title: Further meditation on Will Power
comments.content:
Archdruid,

As you’ve advised I’ve continued my meditation into the manifestation of Will through the element of fire. The elemental barriers to the flow of will are starting to unravel. I didn't fully understand how feeling-emotion is an external force, where as desire-passion are internal. It’s taken me years to accept the idea that my emotions are a part of me, and to have you say that feelings and emotions are external is a pretty terrifying statement. I was thoroughly confused until I tracked down the etymology of the words.

Feeling is the obvious one, it's literally our sensory interaction with the world. It's the gate to our psyche. Emotion means to stir up, or be stirred up. Desire means to want or wish for, and passions means to suffer.

Our cultural obsession with Cacomancy makes it nearly impossible for an individual to develop their Will. Dark spells ensure that blocks and delusion develop at a very young age. From childhood onward we feel pressure that stirs up a deep sense of dissatisfaction to the point of pain, from which we want relief. Our “relief” comes from accepting social patterns that place our self-worth outside our control - do, wear, behave, and speak according to social standards and you will be worthy of societies regard, worthy to participate in the spectacle of life.

There’s enough variation in the doing, wearing, behaving, and speaking that there’s an illusion of free choice, an illusion of control. These choices are all dress up, a menagerie of sub-cultures that ultimately conform to the spectacle.

Instead of achieving the true vision of our personal genius, we play dress-up and go through the motions of achievement but all in a socially acceptable context.

If confidence is trust, and we are taught always to seek validation from outside, then really how can self-trust develop? If our every action our society tells us to take makes us more miserable, when we’re told they’re supposed to make us happier, then how can anything except self-doubt develop?

But there’s a contradiction here, doubt is the alley of the Will when delusion is a barrier. So where is the delusion?

Society blows smoke and calls it fire, we choke upon the smoke and call it happiness. My desire for validation is my desire to choke on the smoke of someone else's fire.

Confidence is to walk clear of the smoke and toward your own fire. The revelries of this great bonfire of theirs are just people stumbling around choking on smoke, no one there can see me clearly enough to give me anything resembling validation. If they can’t see me how can their assessment of me be valid?

You know how to get clear of the smoke? Get on your belly and crawl.

During this process I’ve stumbled upon my Dharma, rendered unto the four elements. To Study, to teach, to lead, to study, and to build. Which falls to which element I do not yet know, but if these are my Dharma, how do I attain them?

My last question for today. Since it is the case that many institutions and individuals are knowingly and unknowingly using Cacomancy against us, doesn’t this constitute a violation of magical ethics? They’re casting upon us without asking our permission, which means they struck first, can we validly retaliate without risking the sticky jam principal?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Further meditation on Will Power
replies.content: Of course it's a violation of magical ethics for the practitioners of cacomagia to do what they're doing. I wouldn't want their karma! Your karma, however, depends on what you do, not what they did. If you're responding to evil with evil, you get the karma of doing evil. There are alternatives -- and some of them are far more powerful.

replies.title: Re: Saturn retrograde
replies.content: The basic rule for a retrograde is that anything that begins with the syllable "re-" is good during one. When Saturn is retrograde it's a good time to rebuild, reestablish, revert, recover, and replace. In particular, it's the best possible time to return to older ways of doing things, since Saturn rules all old things. It's also a good time to retain and reinforce established habits, since Saturn governs the established and the habitual.
